title: Mali crisis deepens as JNIM jihadists begin blockade of Bamako, call for united front against junta
summary: Al-Qaeda affiliated JNIM declared a road blockade on Mali's capital Bamako and called for a broad uprising against the military junta. Hundreds of vehicles remain stranded at entry points, while Russia pledged to maintain its military presence.
contents:
# Mali Crisis Deepens as Jihadists Blockade Bamako

## Situation Update
The Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims (JNIM), an al-Qaeda affiliate, initiated a road blockade on all access routes to Bamako, Mali's capital of 4 million people. The group simultaneously called for a "common front" to topple the military junta.

## Key Developments
- JNIM threatened severe reprisals against anyone traveling on roads leading to Bamako
- Hundreds of passenger and goods vehicles remain stranded at multiple entry points
- Traffic on routes to Conakry, Abidjan, and Dakar - vital port cities for landlocked Mali - thinned dramatically
- JNIM also claimed capture of the Hombori military base in central Mali
- Reports of violence on the Sikasso-Abidjan route with drivers reportedly killed

## Russian Response
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov confirmed Russia would maintain its military presence in Mali to combat extremism and terrorism, despite setbacks including the weekend killing of defense minister Sadio Camara. Analysts noted Russia's image as a security guarantor in Africa has suffered significant damage.

## Strategic Implications
The blockade threatens Mali's economy, heavily dependent on overland trade routes to coastal port cities. Air traffic to Bamako remained unaffected. The crisis represents a significant escalation following coordinated weekend attacks by both jihadist and Tuareg separatist forces.
